title: 15 Tahsas — St. Stephen & The Assembly
narrative:
narrative": "narrative\": \"The fifteenth day of Tahsas honors Saint Stephen, the protomartyr and first deacon of the Church, whose name in the Greek means 'crown' — a name he bore with terrible fidelity, for he was the first to receive the crown of martyrdom for the name of Jesus. Stephen was a man full of faith and the Holy Spirit, chosen with six others to serve the material needs of the community so that the apostles might devote themselves to prayer and the ministry of the word; but the Spirit who filled him did not permit him to confine his ministry to tables of bread, and he proclaimed the Gospel with such power in the synagogues of Jerusalem that his adversaries, unable to resist his wisdom, dragged him before the Sanhedrin. His defense speech, the longest recorded in the Acts of the Apostles, recapitulated the entire history of Israel as a history of divine condescension meeting human rejection — a history now reaching its climax in the rejection of the Messiah. He was stoned to death outside the walls of Jerusalem, praying for his murderers in the words of his Lord.

Saint Gregory the Illuminator, whose departure from this life is commemorated on this day, was the apostle and first catholicos of Armenia, a man whose name in the Greek means 'watchful' or 'he who watches.' Gregory was imprisoned for thirteen years in a pit called Khor Virap by King Tiridates III of Armenia, whom he had refused to honor with pagan sacrifice; when Tiridates was struck with madness, Gregory was released and healed him by prayer, an event that led to the conversion of the Armenian kingdom and its adoption of Christianity as the state religion in the year three hundred and one, making Armenia the first nation in history to embrace the Christian faith officially.

Saint Eleutherius of Rome, a second-century pope whose name in the Greek means 'free' or 'the liberator,' completes this day's assembly. Eleutherius guided the Church of Rome through a period of relative peace and consolidation, receiving delegations from the nascent Christian community in Britain and exercising the pastoral care of a bishop who understood his office as one of service to the freedom of the Gospel in an often hostile world. His name 'liberator' connects him to the great theme of the Incarnation itself, for Christ came to proclaim liberty to the captives and the opening of the prison to those who were bound.

The theological seal of this fifteenth day of Tahsas is the mystery of witness at the midpoint of the month. The month of the Incarnation reaches its midpoint on this day; the voices of expectation have been sounding for fifteen days, and fifteen more days of increasing intensity remain before the great feast. Stephen, Gregory, and Eleutherius each in their own way stand at a hinge point: Stephen at the hinge between the Jewish and Christian dispensations, Gregory at the hinge between a pagan and a Christian Armenia, Eleutherius at the hinge between persecution and consolidation. The God who stands at all hinges and opens all doors is the God who on the twenty-fifth of this month will open the door of heaven and descend into human flesh.
